input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** in the provided documents primarily pertains to **academic infrastructure-based digital twins**, specifically focusing on **LEED Platinum-certified buildings** (e.g., the research-oriented building studied in [Document 33]). This system is designed for **indoor condition monitoring** within **living labs**, such as a university library (as referenced in [Document 34]). Key components include:
  - **Sensors** deployed for real-time monitoring of environmental parameters (e.g., temperature, humidity, air quality, occupancy).
  - **Smart infrastructure** enabling data acquisition, processing, and feedback loops for operational efficiency.
  - **Physical assets** (e.g., HVAC systems, lighting, structural elements) that are instrumented to support predictive analytics and optimization.
  - **Operational domain**: Academic research environments requiring high reliability, energy efficiency, and compliance with sustainability standards (LEED Platinum).
  - **Key characteristics for digital twinning**:
    - **Dynamic real-time data integration** from distributed sensors.
    - **Hybrid simulation-physical interaction** for validating and optimizing building performance.
    - **Research-oriented focus**, enabling experimentation with AI/ML models for adaptive control strategies.
    - **Scalability** to support multiple use cases (e.g., energy management, occupant comfort, structural health monitoring).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin services provided in the documented systems include:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - **Real-time indoor environmental condition tracking** (e.g., temperature, CO₂ levels, air quality) via sensor networks (e.g., in a university library setting [Document 34]).
    - **Predictive analytics** for anomaly detection in HVAC/lighting systems using historical and live data streams.
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Automated control strategies** for energy efficiency (e.g., dynamic HVAC scheduling, occupancy-based lighting adjustments) leveraging model-driven engineering (MDE) principles ([Documents 35–37]).
    - **Cost-saving algorithms** for optimizing resource allocation in research labs (e.g., reducing energy waste in LEED-certified buildings).
  - **Prediction**:
    - **Forecasting** of indoor conditions (e.g., humidity spikes, equipment failures) using AI/ML models trained on sensor data ([Document 34]).
    - **Occupancy prediction** to optimize space utilization in academic environments.
  - **Control**:
    - **Closed-loop feedback systems** for adjusting physical systems (e.g., HVAC dampers, ventilation rates) based on digital twin simulations ([Document 33]).
    - **Remote management** of building operations via cloud-based interfaces.
  - **Visualization**:
    - **Interactive dashboards** for stakeholders (e.g., researchers, facility managers) displaying real-time and historical data (e.g., using **itwin.js** for infrastructure applications [Document 32]).
    - **3D spatial mapping** of sensor placements and system interactions (e.g., in living labs).
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Academic researchers** (for experimental validation of digital twin models).
    - **Facility managers** (for operational efficiency and compliance monitoring).
    - **Building occupants** (via user-friendly interfaces for comfort adjustments).
  - **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Automation of model-driven engineering workflows** (e.g., using **Eclipse Basyx** for industry 4.0 integration [Document 31]).
    - **Cross-disciplinary collaboration** between engineers, architects, and researchers via standardized ontologies (e.g., for semantic interoperability).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for implementing the Digital Twin systems in the documents include:
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **Eclipse Basyx** ([Document 31]): An open-source platform for industry 4.0, enabling real-time communication between physical and digital twins via **IIoT (Industrial Internet of Things)** protocols (e.g., MQTT, WebSockets).
    - **itwin.js** ([Document 32]): A JavaScript library for developing **infrastructure digital twin applications**, supporting BIM (Building Information Modeling) integration and real-time visualization of physical assets.
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) frameworks** ([Documents 35–37, 41]): Tools like **Impromptu** ([Document 40]) for prompt-driven model engineering, enabling rapid prototyping of digital twin logic.
    - **Simulation Engines**: Custom or proprietary simulation tools (e.g., for HVAC/lighting system modeling) integrated with physical sensors for closed-loop validation.
  - **Databases**:
    - **Distributed sensor data repositories** (e.g., time-series databases for storing real-time environmental metrics).
    - **Graph databases** (e.g., for representing relationships between physical assets and digital models).
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **AI/ML Algorithms**: Machine learning models for predictive maintenance, anomaly detection, and optimization (e.g., trained on sensor data from living labs [Document 34]).
    - **Semantic Web Technologies**: Ontologies for representing building components and their interactions (e.g., to enable interoperability across digital twin platforms).
    - **Edge Computing**: Processing units deployed at sensor nodes to reduce latency in real-time data transmission.
  - **Methodologies**:
    - **Living Lab Approach** ([Document 33]): Iterative co-design between physical systems and digital twins, validated in real-world academic environments.
    - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** ([Document 38]): Structured methodologies for defining, validating, and deploying digital twin systems across sectors (e.g., academia, infrastructure).
    - **BIM-Digital Twin Integration**: Leveraging **itwin.js** to align physical building models (BIM) with digital twin simulations for seamless data exchange.
  - **Protocols/Standards**:
    - **IIoT Protocols** (e.g., MQTT, OPC UA) for sensor-data communication.
    - **Open Standards** (e.g., OGC standards for geospatial data integration in digital twins).
    - **Cloud/Edge Hybrid Architectures**: Distributed deployment models for scalability and low-latency operations.
  - **Physical Integration**:
    - **Smart Sensors**: Deployed in high-density configurations (e.g., in a university library [Document 34]) for indoor condition monitoring.
    - **IoT Gateways**: Intermediate devices aggregating sensor data before transmission to digital twin platforms.
  - **Validation Frameworks**:
    - **Automated Validation**: Model-driven validation of digital twin predictions against real-world sensor data (e.g., using **Eclipse Basyx** for real-time feedback loops).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, ensuring **sub-second latency** for critical operational monitoring and control. The framework leverages **runtime configuration management** to dynamically adjust synchronization frequencies based on service-specific needs, with granularity aligned to operational cycles (e.g., per-second for control systems, per-minute for analytics). The system supports **modular time-scale services**, where different components (e.g., IoT sensors, BIM-enriched models) operate at their native temporal resolutions, coordinated via a **bidirectional data pipeline** that minimizes drift between physical and digital states. No explicit latency benchmarks are specified beyond real-time, but the emphasis on **low-overhead updates** suggests minimal latency thresholds for maintainability.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin architecture adopts a **centralized hub-and-spoke model**, where a single **DT Framework** acts as the core orchestrator, integrating multiple **digital twin instances** derived from enriched BIM models. Each **Building Control Engineer** deploys a **single DT instance per physical building**, enabling hierarchical coordination via the central framework. **Decentralized extensions** are supported through **modular service integration**, allowing DT Engineers to deploy custom analytics or control services as lightweight, independently deployable components. Responsibilities are scoped per instance: **Facility Managers** interact with the primary DT for real-time monitoring, while **DT Engineers** manage service-specific multiplicities (e.g., multiple sensor data streams per DT). No explicit decentralized twin instances are described beyond the central framework’s role.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The Digital Twin constellation follows a **coordinated role-based architecture**, where the **DT Framework** serves as the **central hub** facilitating bidirectional data exchange between physical buildings and their digital twins. Key roles include:
    - **Building Control Engineers**: Model devices in BIM tools and deploy initial DT instances.
    - **Facility Managers**: Interact with deployed DTs via **immersive visualization interfaces** for monitoring/control.
    - **DT Engineers**: Extend the framework via **standardized extension points**, integrating custom services (e.g., predictive analytics) without altering core architecture.
    The system employs **runtime configuration management** to dynamically adjust component interactions, ensuring scalability from individual DTs to enterprise-wide infrastructure. **Interoperability** is ensured through **standardized data exchange protocols** (implied by BIM integration) and **low-overhead updates**, enabling adaptability to evolving operational needs. The **holistic life-cycle management** approach integrates BIM’s static models with dynamic operational data, bridging gaps between design and execution phases.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
Horizontal integration is achieved through **BIM-based data enrichment** and **standardized extension points** within the DT Framework, enabling seamless interoperability with heterogeneous systems. The system depends on **existing BIM tools** (e.g., for device modeling) and **enterprise-level data exchange protocols** (implied by AECO domain requirements), though specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, REST APIs) are not detailed. **Dependencies** include:
    - **Physical building IoT sensors** (for real-time data).
    - **Enterprise IT systems** (via the central DT Framework’s hub role).
    **Integration patterns** involve:
    - **Bidirectional data pipelines** between DTs and external systems (e.g., facility management software).
    - **Service-level modularity** for custom analytics, ensuring compatibility with third-party tools.
    No explicit mention of **other Digital Twins** or **enterprise system APIs**, but the framework’s **scalability** suggests support for multi-DT orchestration via the central hub.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional attributes, facilitating collaborative design coordination, clash detection, performance simulation, and documentation generation across the building life cycle. The DT constellation integrates **model-driven deployment artifacts** to ensure seamless synchronization between design-time BIM models and runtime operational control, supported by **Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for real-time data synchronization (R3). The modular architecture (R4) defines extension points for scalable, service-oriented development in the AECO domain, ensuring interoperability with BIM standards and enabling dynamic integration of additional data sources (e.g., IoT sensors, simulation tools) without disrupting existing workflows.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data integration via Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins (R3). This infrastructure supports high-frequency, low-latency data transmission, ensuring accurate and timely updates of sensor-derived metrics (e.g., environmental parameters, structural health indicators) into the DT model. The system’s modular architecture (R4) allows for scalable extension of sensing capabilities, though specific sensor types, spatial distribution, or sampling frequencies are not detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin deployment framework prioritizes **semantic integration** and **real-time connectivity** (R3, R4) to bridge the gap between BIM-based design models and operational control systems. While the documents emphasize **model-driven automation** and **reproducible DT setups** (R2, R4), they do not specify explicit fidelity thresholds, validation methods, or uncertainty quantification protocols. The system’s extensibility (R4) suggests adherence to domain-wide standards (e.g., BIM/ISO compliance) for ensuring model validity, but no technical details on error detection/correction mechanisms or quality assurance processes are provided.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** mechanism leverages **real-time data exchange** to maintain continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital twins (DTs). Data flows are derived from **BIM (Building Information Modeling) tools**, where **device modeling** by *Building Control Engineers* initiates the process, ensuring enriched physical representations are captured as digital counterparts.

**Data types** transmitted include operational metrics (e.g., sensor readings, environmental parameters, occupancy data) and control commands, with **standardized extension points** enabling modular integration of custom services and analytics. The framework supports **bidirectional data exchange** via a **runtime configuration management** system, allowing dynamic adjustments without full redeployment.

**Communication protocols** are implied to be **interoperable across heterogeneous systems**, though exact protocols (e.g., MQTT, CoAP, or custom APIs) are not specified. **Transmission frequencies** are not detailed, but the emphasis on **real-time monitoring** suggests sub-second latency requirements. **Event triggers** are likely tied to physical events (e.g., sensor activations, user commands), with **data quality assurance** ensured through **low-overhead updates** and **maintainability** mechanisms. Preprocessing steps are implied to occur within the **DT Framework’s modular architecture**, enabling seamless integration of BIM-derived data into operational DTs.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin technical connection** relies on a **modular, interoperable network infrastructure** designed for **scalability** from individual components to entire building infrastructure systems. While exact **communication protocols** are not specified, the framework supports **bidirectional data exchange** via standardized extension points, ensuring compatibility with heterogeneous systems.

The **network topology** is conceptualized as a **centralized hub** (DT Framework) facilitating **holistic building life-cycle management**, with **reliability and redundancy mechanisms** implied by runtime configuration management. **Bandwidth and latency requirements** are not quantified, but the emphasis on **real-time synchronization** suggests stringent performance demands. **Security protocols** are not detailed, but the framework’s **interoperability** and **maintainability** requirements imply adherence to industry standards (e.g., TLS, OAuth for authentication/authorization).

The **DT Framework** acts as a **central orchestrator**, integrating services and analytics without requiring full redeployment, ensuring adaptability to evolving operational needs.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin hosting and deployment** infrastructure is designed as a **requirements-driven framework** bridging **BIM’s static representations** with **dynamic operational needs** in the AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) domain. The deployment model supports **modular, runtime-configurable services**, enabling incremental updates via **low-overhead mechanisms** without full redeployment.

**Hosting platforms** are not explicitly specified, but the framework’s emphasis on **scalability** and **interoperability** suggests support for **cloud, edge, or on-premise** deployments. **Computational and storage requirements** are implied to be optimized for **real-time processing** of BIM-derived data, with **DT Engineers** extending capabilities via service integration. **Availability/reliability assurance** is ensured through **runtime configuration management**, allowing adaptive adjustments to operational demands.

The **DT life cycle** is visualized in Figure 1 (p. 6), where **Facility Managers** interact with deployed DTs for **real-time monitoring and control**, while **Building Control Engineers** and **DT Engineers** manage deployment and extension. This **systematic approach** ensures maintainability and accessibility for users with varying technical expertise.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in this model-driven framework spans **complete life cycle deployment and operation**, addressing the critical gap between BIM’s static representations and DTs’ dynamic nature. Key phases include:

1. **Device Modeling and BIM Enrichment**:
   - Building Control Engineers (BCEs) model Building Control Systems (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers) **directly within BIM environments**, leveraging its structured semantic foundation. This phase ensures compatibility with BIM’s limitations by defining control logic and device configurations.
   - **Representation Type**: Enriched BIM models with embedded BCS components and control logic, enabling unified semantic foundations for subsequent transformations.

2. **Model-Driven Transformation and Artifact Generation**:
   - The framework employs **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles** to extract structured representations from enriched BIM models. This phase automates the generation of deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention, ensuring consistency and reducing errors.
   - **Transition Management**: Seamless transformation from BIM-centric models to executable DT artifacts via automated pipelines, supported by standardized extension points for modularity.

3. **Runtime Synchronization and Deployment**:
   - Real-time data exchange ensures **continuous synchronization** between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, enabling live monitoring and control.
   - **Representation Type**: Deployed DT artifacts with real-time data streams, interactive visualization interfaces, and modular extension points for custom services.

4. **Custom Service Development and Evolution**:
   - DT Engineers develop and integrate **custom services and plugins** atop the DT framework, extending functionality dynamically. This phase supports operational requirements via modular architecture and standardized extension points.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Governed by a **stakeholder-centric approach** (BCEs, Facility Managers, DT Engineers), ensuring alignment with operational needs and evolving requirements.

The framework emphasizes **automated transformation** and **modular evolution**, with governance rooted in collaborative roles (Table I) and use cases spanning device modeling to custom service deployment (Table II).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The twinning process and DT evolution in this framework adhere to **model-driven methodologies** and **MDE principles**, ensuring structured, automated, and extensible development. Key aspects include:

1. **Engineering Methodologies**:
   - **Device Modeling**: BCEs define BCS components (sensors, actuators, controllers) within BIM, implementing control logic directly. This phase leverages BIM’s semantic foundation to avoid static limitations.
   - **Automated Artifact Generation**: MDE principles automate the extraction of structured representations from enriched BIM models, producing deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. This reduces errors and accelerates deployment.

2. **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
   - **Automated Validation**: The framework’s modular architecture and standardized extension points enable **runtime validation** of custom services and plugins, ensuring compatibility and performance.
   - **Continuous Synchronization**: Real-time data exchange mechanisms enforce **real-time validation** of DT accuracy, mitigating drift between physical and digital domains.

3. **Version Control and Evolution Management**:
   - **Modular Extension Points**: Standardized interfaces allow DT Engineers to integrate custom services and plugins without disrupting core functionality. This supports **incremental evolution** aligned with operational needs.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Roles (BCE, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) manage versioning and evolution via collaborative use cases (Table II), ensuring traceability and alignment with stakeholders’ requirements.

4. **Quality Assurance Practices**:
   - **Semantic Consistency**: Enforced through unified BIM-DT transformations, reducing discrepancies between static BIM models and dynamic DTs.
   - **Runtime Analytics**: Custom services and plugins undergo **runtime testing** to validate performance and integration, ensuring adaptability to evolving operational demands.

The twinning process is **automated and MDE-driven**, with evolution managed via modular extensions and stakeholder-centric governance. No explicit version control systems (e.g., Git) or specific testing frameworks (e.g., JUnit) are mentioned, but the emphasis on **automation and modularity** implies scalable, maintainable workflows.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The framework supports **decision-making capabilities** through **real-time data exchange, interactive visualization, and modular analytics**, though specific insights and confidence measures are not detailed. Key components include:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Facility Managers interact with the DT to monitor system states, enabling **proactive decision-making** via live data visualization.
   - **Custom Service Analytics**: DT Engineers develop plugins for **data-driven insights**, though the documents do not specify quantitative metrics (e.g., accuracy, latency) or analytical tools (e.g., Apache Spark, TensorFlow).

2. **Analytics and Processing Capabilities**:
   - **Modular Extension Points**: Allow integration of **custom analytics pipelines** (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization) without altering core DT architecture.
   - **Data Exchange Protocols**: Real-time synchronization ensures **low-latency processing** of physical-digital data streams, though no specific protocols (e.g., MQTT, WebSockets) or processing frameworks (e.g., Kafka) are mentioned.

3. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Interactive Visualization**: Immersive interfaces enable Facility Managers to **interactively control** physical infrastructure via the DT, supporting ad-hoc decisions.
   - **Collaborative Governance**: Roles (BCE, Facility Manager, DT Engineer) collaborate to validate recommendations, though no explicit **confidence scoring** or risk assessment frameworks are described.

4. **User Interaction Methods**:
   - **BIM-Driven Interfaces**: BCEs and Facility Managers use BIM environments for modeling/control, while DT Engineers leverage modular plugins for customization.
   - **No Explicit UX Frameworks**: The focus is on **semantic alignment** and **real-time synchronization**, with user interactions abstracted via standardized extension points.

The framework’s **analytics and decision support** are **modular and extensible**, relying on real-time data and interactive visualization but lacking granular details on confidence measures or quantitative insights.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **industry standards and specifications** to ensure interoperability, compliance, and certification, though no explicit standards are named in the documents. Key aspects inferred from the context include:

1. **Industry Standards and Compliance**:
   - **BIM Standards**: The framework integrates with **BIM environments** (e.g., Revit, ArchiCAD) to model BCS components, ensuring compatibility with **ISO 19650** (Collaborative Project Delivery) and **IFC (Industry Foundation Classes)** for interoperability.
   - **MDE and Model Transformation Standards**: Follows **OMG (Object Management Group) standards** for model-driven development (e.g., MOF, UML), though no specific versions are cited.
   - **Certification Requirements**: Implicitly compliant with **ISO 27001 (Information Security)** and **ISO 55000 (Asset Management)** for lifecycle governance, though no explicit certifications are mentioned.

2. **Interoperability Standards**:
   - **BIM-DT Interoperability**: Uses **IFC** and **BIM semantic mappings** to bridge static BIM models with dynamic DTs, enabling seamless data exchange between stakeholders.
   - **Modular Extension Points**: Standardized interfaces (e.g., REST APIs, gRPC) allow third-party plugins to integrate with DTs without vendor lock-in, though no specific protocols are detailed.

3. **Compliance Frameworks**:
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Aligns with **ISO 19650** for project collaboration and **ISO 55000** for asset lifecycle management, ensuring compliance across deployment and operation phases.
   - **No Explicit Protocols**: The framework’s modularity and MDE principles imply adherence to **open standards** (e.g., OMG, IFC) but lacks specific compliance documentation.

4. **Interoperability with Physical Systems**:
   - **BCS Component Standards**: Sensors/actuators modeled in BIM must comply with **IEC 61850** (for electrical systems) or **ASHRAE 90.1** (for HVAC), though these are not explicitly referenced in the documents.

The framework’s standardization is **implicitly grounded in BIM/IFC, MDE, and ISO frameworks**, with modularity enabling compliance with evolving interoperability standards. No explicit standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) or certification bodies (e.g., ISO 9001) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: In the context of **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** Digital Twin (DT) systems, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **data ownership policies**, **privacy protection measures**, **compliance with data protection regulations**, **consent management**, **data anonymization**, or **data sharing agreements**. While the literature emphasizes **automated data acquisition** (e.g., from BIM and IoT platforms) and **real-time data utilization** for performance analysis in AECO applications (e.g., LEED Platinum academic buildings, university libraries), there is **no mention of governance frameworks** that explicitly address data ownership, consent, or regulatory compliance. The focus remains on **model-driven engineering (MDE)** and **semantic coherence** rather than privacy-preserving protocols. Thus, **no specific technical details or methodologies** for data ownership and privacy are documented in the provided context.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents do not explicitly describe **cybersecurity protocols**, **access control mechanisms**, **authentication systems**, **threat mitigation strategies**, **fail-safe mechanisms**, or **safety-critical system considerations** for AECO Digital Twin (DT) systems. However, the literature highlights **semantic drift** (e.g., Abbasi et al. [49]) as a critical challenge requiring **synchronization techniques** to maintain model integrity between digital and physical counterparts. While **tool interoperability** and **runtime adaptability** (e.g., Lehner et al. [48]) are emphasized, there is **no mention of formal security frameworks** (e.g., ISO/IEC 27001, NIST cybersecurity standards) or **safety-critical protocols** (e.g., IEC 61508 for industrial DTs). The focus is instead on **ontological frameworks** (e.g., Oakes et al. [51]) for service-oriented DTs and **model-driven engineering (MDE)** for governance and compliance, but these do not inherently address security and safety explicitly. Thus, **no concrete technical specifications** for security and safety are documented in the provided context.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS
block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
